Driverless 18-wheelers may reach your street sooner than you think
Opening Bid Unfiltered is available on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, YouTube, or wherever you get your podcasts. The driverless car race is well underway. While Waymo is seen as having a leadership position, Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk is aiming for robotaxi debuts likely in 2026. Thus far, the race to bring autonomous trucks to life is a bit more open, with companies seeking to improve logistics efficiency. Aurora Innovation (AUR) is seen as the leader at this juncture and is backed by Uber (UBER), boasting key partnerships with Paccar (PCAR), Nvidia (NVDA), and Volvo (VOLCAR-B.ST). The company has successfully debuted driverless commercial trucks on select routes in Texas, with plans to expand to more routes in 2026. Yahoo Finance Executive Editor Brian Sozzi talks on the Opening Bid Unfiltered podcast with Aurora Innovation co-founder and Chris Urmson. Urmson led Google's (GOOG) driverless technology unit for several years, which ultimately became Waymo. He shares his vision for Aurora Innovation, the 2026 product roadmap, and the potential benefits of autonomous technology for commercial trucks. Listen on your favorite podcast platform or watch on our website for full episodes of Opening Bid Unfiltered. Yahoo Finance's Opening Bid Unfiltered is produced by Langston Sessoms.

DETROIT (Reuters) -General Motors is cutting output at one of its main electric-vehicle factories, the latest automaker to pull back on EVs as intensifying competition and shifting consumer preferences pressure the market for green cars. GM will stop production of two electric Cadillac SUVs at its assembly plant in Spring Hill, Tennessee, during the month of December, according to a person familiar with the matter and communications to GM employees viewed by Reuters. The plant produces the midsize Cadillac Lyriq - a relative hit and one of GM's top-selling EVs - and the Vistiq, a larger electric SUV.

Wall Street indexes post record-high closes; Tesla and Micron rally
Wall Street’s main indexes notched record-high closes on Thursday, with the Dow adding about 2.7%, the S&P 500 climbing more than 1.6%, and the Nasdaq rising about 1.4%. U.S. consumer prices rose more than expected in August, and the annual increase in inflation was the largest in over a year. A separate report showed initial jobless claims increased for the week ended September 6 to the highest level in nearly six years. Sam Stovall, chief investment strategist with CFRA Research, said markets cheered the latest data, which ensures the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its meeting later this month. “Well, the stocks are up across the board today, I think primarily because the weaker-than-expected employment report really is now causing investors to say a September cut is assured. But also, because we had slightly warmer month-on-month readings for CPI, the chance of a 50-basis-point cut has been taken off the table, but a third cut has been added — meaning 35 basis points in September, October, and December. So those three factors together I think have caused investors not only to cover whatever shorts there might have been, but also add new money.” Shares of Tesla boosted markets with a 12% gain. Micron Technology shares jumped 14% after Citigroup raised its price target on the memory chipmaker’s stock. And shares of Warner Brothers Discovery leapt 58% after the Wall Street Journal reported that Paramount Skydance is preparing a majority cash bid for the struggling media company.

text: Analysis-BYD and Tesla set to lose most from Mexico's proposed tariffs on China (Reuters) -Electric car rivals BYD and Tesla are set to be the biggest losers from Mexico’s proposed 50 percent tariff on autos imported from China, following recent shifts in global currency markets that have increased the relative cost of Chinese exports. The proposed tariff, announced on Wednesday, targets electric and gasoline cars imported from all countries that Mexico doesn’t have a free trade agreement with, including South Korea, India, Indonesia and Russia. In practice, however, the tariff would mostly impact electric cars manufactured in China and sold in Mexico, industry analysts said.

Wall Street indexes post record-high closes; Tesla and Micron rally
Wall Street’s main indexes notched record-high closes on Thursday, with the Dow adding about 2.7%, the S&P 500 climbing more than 1.6%, and the Nasdaq rising about 1.4%. U.S. consumer prices rose more than expected in August, and the annual increase in inflation was the largest in over a year. A separate report showed initial jobless claims increased for the week ended September 6 to the highest level in nearly six years. Sam Stovall, chief investment strategist with CFRA Research, said markets cheered the latest data, which ensures the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its meeting later this month. “Well, the stocks are up across the board today, I think primarily because the weaker-than-expected employment report really is now causing investors to say a September cut is assured. But also, because we had slightly warmer month-on-month readings for CPI, the chance of a 50-basis-point cut has been taken off the table, but a third cut has been added — meaning 35 basis points in September, October, and December. So those three factors together I think have caused investors not only to cover whatever shorts there might have been, but also add new money.” Shares of Tesla boosted markets with a 12% gain. Micron Technology shares jumped 14% after Citigroup raised its price target on the memory chipmaker’s stock. And shares of Warner Brothers Discovery leapt 58% after the Wall Street Journal reported that Paramount Skydance is preparing a majority cash bid for the struggling media company.

McDonald's global sales surged past estimates as promotions ignited strong demand, with the company on Wednesday reporting second-quarter earnings and revenue that decisively beat Wall Street expectations, fueling a robust three and a half percent jump in shares during morning trading. The fast-food giant capitalized on the success of its promotions and affordable meal bundles, drawing in budget-conscious customers eager to maximize value amid ongoing economic uncertainty. Competition in the fast-food sector has intensified, as McDonald's and rivals Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands aggressively ramp up value meals to combat a slowdown in customer demand. In April, McDonald's launched a limited-time Happy Meal tied to the "Minecraft" movie, followed by the May introduction of McCrispy Chicken Strips as a permanent menu item. Menu innovation and strategic promotions also powered Domino's to report upbeat U.S. same-store sales, while softening demand weighed on results at Yum Brands and Chipotle. McDonald's emphasized that re-engaging low-income consumers is "critical," as they make up the majority of its foot traffic, though the CEO cautioned that executives remain vigilant regarding the overall near-term health of the U.S. consumer.

text: Analysis-BYD and Tesla set to lose most from Mexico's proposed tariffs on China (Reuters) -Electric car rivals BYD and Tesla are set to be the biggest losers from Mexico’s proposed 50 percent tariff on autos imported from China, following recent shifts in global currency markets that have increased the relative cost of Chinese exports. This move is expected to deliver a likely blow to the fast-growing electric car market in Mexico while sparing the traditional "Big Three" U.S. car manufacturers. The proposed tariff, announced on Wednesday, targets electric and gasoline cars imported from all countries that Mexico doesn’t have a free trade agreement with, including South Korea, India, Indonesia and Russia. In practice, however, the tariff would mostly impact electric cars manufactured in China and sold in Mexico, industry analysts said.
